data.title: Trillion Dollar Market Cap Changing Of The Guard
data.description:
For the past several months, NVIDIA (NVDA) has held the title of the world's largest stock by market cap. While NVDA has pulled back from a peak market cap just above $5 trillion in late October/early November to $4.5 trillion today, there's still a significant gap versus the next largest stock: Alphabet (GOOGL).

GOOGL in the #2 spot is a new development. As shown below, it has typically been either Apple (AAPL) or Microsoft (MSFT) holding that position in recent years; however, both of those stocks have pulled back as GOOGL has pressed to new highs. As shown in the second chart below, this is the first time that AAPL is smaller than GOOGL since January 29, 2019. Additionally, we would note that AAPL is currently larger than Microsoft, but that gap has also been narrowing. In the past several years, the two stocks have repeatedly gone back and forth for the larger market cap of the two.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Neogen: Starting 2026 With A Bang
data.description:
In October I concluded that Neogen (NEOG), itself being a food safety play, was still not a safety play itself, at least for investors. Following a well-documented and incredibly value-destructive 3M Food Safety acquisition/merger, the business has suffered from a weak performance and high leverage for years.

A recent divestment was welcomed to tackle debt, and while cost-cutting moves were announced, profits were elusive. Having initiated a long position before, I noticed the potential but failed to see sufficient green shots to add to that position. By now green shoots have arrived, triggering a massive move higher in the shares on the back of relatively modest numerical improvements, but the direction of travel changed, and that is key now.

Anything But A Safety Play

Neogen was founded in the 1980s with a goal to create a bright future for global food security, with people and animals having access to a food supply that is safe, sufficient, and sustainable. Providing such services includes producing products and services like indicator testing, pathogens, hygiene monitoring, animal care, veterinary instruments, etc. These revenues are split pretty evenly between North America and the rest of the world.

Many of these positives are so-called consumables, often relating to testing, with relatively low cost of tests but a critical need to perform such tasks, which in theory creates a very defensive positioning.

Just a $5 stock in 2010, the company was a real investor darling in the 2010s as shares rose to levels in the $40s later in the decade as the company delivered on solid growth and margins. Over the past decade, the company grew sales from $300 million in 2015 to $500 million in 2022, with GAAP operating margins reported well in the teens.

That thesis changed overnight as the company announced a huge $5 billion deal to acquire 3M Food Safety, a deal designed to double the sales base to $1 billion. This all took place after management loaded up with quite some confidence, this being a $50 stock in 2021.

Just Disappointments

The pro forma billion-dollar business, upon the deal announcement, ended up posting sales of just $924 million in the fiscal year 2024. Worse, EBITDA of $213 million fell short of a $300 million promised number, and there was the issue with net debt reported exceeding $700 million.

Even worse, the company guided for 2025 sales to fall just below $900 million, with EBITDA seen just below $200 million, as a $50 stock was down to $5 in April of last year, for a $1.9 billion enterprise valuation. This was very painful, as the company paid $5 billion for just part of the business just a couple of years before! However, the company already reduced its footprint, announcing a $130 million deal to sell its Cleaners & Disinfectants business, bringing in some much-needed cash to address leverage.

That move, taking place at acceptable revenue multiples, would help to reduce leverage ratios a bit, but over the fall, shares were stuck around $6 per share. 2025 sales were down another 3% to $895 million, with EBITDA reported at just $184 million, hardly inspiring. The real issue is that sales were seen down to a midpoint of $830 million in 2026, with EBITDA seen down to $170 million, as some of these declines could be attributed to the sale of the Cleaners & Disinfectants business, but not all.

In October, the company reported a 4% fall in first-quarter sales to $209 million, as net debt was down to $661 million, for a 3.6 times leverage ratio based on the EBITDA outlook. The issue is that while EBITDA numbers were posted, adjusted earnings came in at a paltry four cents, these furthermore being highly adjusted numbers, this limiting my enthusiasm to add to a modest long position here.

Starting 2026 On A Strong Note

After making two leadership appointments in the first week of trading, the company reported encouraging second-quarter results. While reported second-quarter sales were down 3% to $225 million, core sales growth, so excluding the divestment, was up nearly 3%. Adjusted earnings were reported at $0.10 per share, down a penny on the year before but up six cents from the first quarter!

This profit number is equal to about $22 million in dollar terms, still standing in sharp contrast to a near $16 million GAAP loss, that being largely due to amortization charges. Other charges are quite frequent, often involve cash payments, and not all adjustments can be accepted on management's blue eyes, including, of course, modest stock-based compensation expenses.

The big game changer is that after years of revenue declines, the company upped the full-year guidance and now sees sales around $850 million. This marks a $20 million increase from the previous guidance. It appears that finally some momentum has arrived for the business, driven by hard internal work.

EBITDA is now seen around $175 million, a $5 million improvement on that front compared to the previous guidance. As net debt is reported at $655 million, leverage remains elevated at 3.7 times, but the direction of travel is positive, that is for sure, as the company furthermore confirmed on the call that it is exploring alternatives for its genomics business.

Updating The Take

The 271 million shares outstanding of Neogen popped from nearly $7.50 to nearly $10 per share upon the news announcement, adding half a billion in value on the back of the positive story. By now the market value has jumped to $2.1 billion, for a near $2.8 billion enterprise valuation.

At this point, the enterprise valuation has risen to over 3 times sales over 15 times EBITDA, with realistic earnings still not very noteworthy, as investors for now are relieved that the worst is over. If the company can return to posting realistic operating profits of 15%-20% on a $900 million revenue base, operating profits of $135-$180 million might result in net earnings between $75 million and $110 million, equal to about $0.35-$0.50 per share.

This means that some good news has been priced in, and while the company commanded much higher multiples in the 2010s, a lot of the track record has been wiped out recently.

Frankly, the re-rating is quite substantial, with shares up two-thirds since October, following a monstrous rally in just three months. However, the pace of improvements is dramatic, with EBITDA margins up 5 points in just a quarter!

Weighing all this, I remain constructive but more cautious on the shares, just as the green shoots have come through, causing shares to have been repriced quite aggressively. For now I will "Hold" on to the shares, but if shares might really rally to the lower teens, I am definitely considering trimming some of the position.

data.title: Greenbrier Companies: Run With House Money
data.description:
We last issued a Buy call on The Greenbrier Companies, Inc. (GBX) stock in April of 2025. Shares have rallied some 30% from there. In past trades, we have consistently maintained that when you buy in the 20s and 30s, moving to a Hold rating makes sense when you get into the $50s. Given the price action we have seen, we do believe moving to a hold makes sense here at about $52 a share. We think if you took our trade, then running a house position is a good idea given the buybacks and dividends. By a house position, we mean taking an approach of selling out your initial investment plus 25%-50% of the profit and letting the rest run long-term as an investment. We teach this strategy to our investing group members at BAD BEAT Investing with the goal of laying the groundwork for long-term wealth creation.

GBX makes a good candidate here. By taking this approach, you will have exposure to all future gains in the stock but, more importantly, access to the dividends, any spinoffs, and benefits of share repurchases. With the principal and a chunk of the profit now in cash, we then move on to better near-term opportunities for gains like you will have locked in with GBX. That said, we like the operations and momentum here, and today, we check back in on GBX. Let us discuss fiscal Q1 results and the outlook for the year ahead.

Greenbrier Companies Fiscal Q1 Headline Earnings Results

In the just-reported fiscal quarter, the top-line revenue figure was actually ahead of expectations for revenue. Q1 revenues were down some 20% from last year to $706 million. This was driven by lower deliveries and fewer new railcar orders. While the lease fleet has grown, clearly looking at the trend of the last few quarters, there is some pressure. The backlog is still healthy, and despite lower revenue, the margins and core EBITDA reflect solid expense management. We saw strong earnings on the bottom line relative to expectations. The adjusted EPS of $1.14 surpassed our expectations of $0.90-$1.00 in earnings per share and beat the Street consensus by $0.35.

Lorie Tekorius, CEO and President, stated in the earnings press release:

Greenbrier delivered solid results in Q1. Leasing and Fleet Management provided stability through strong execution and recurring cash flows including selectively recycling capital through fleet sales in a strong equipment market to support liquidity and balance sheet strength. Manufacturing achieved good operating performance on lower volumes"

While operational performance was a bit better than expected, gross margin overall was 14.6% in Q1, down 430 basis points from the sequential quarter. The operating margin of 8.7% was also down 80 basis points. Lower production and maintenance volumes impacted margins.

Segment Summary

Greenbrier is now a manufacturing and fleet management segment. Both segments were a bit lighter than the sequential Q4 as expected, though overall performance was better than expected. Gross margin was 11.0%, down from 15.7% in the manufacturing segment in the linked quarter. This was a result of operating inefficiencies in production rates and throughput in new railcar facilities. There were also fewer deliveries, as was expected and planned. Operating margin was down to 7.4%, versus 10.8% in the sequential Q4.

In the leasing and fleet management services segment, gross margins slipped due to less fleet income. Gross margin was 63.5% versus 64.9% in the sequential Q4. However, operating margin benefited from a bounce in equipment sales and hit 89.6%, up dramatically from 53.0% in Q4. While such a margin cannot be sustained, it helped drive earnings from operations in this segment higher and led to a better-than-expected earnings result, as we noted above.

Order Update

New railcar orders in fiscal Q1 totaled 3,700 units, and these were valued at $550 million. Deliveries were lower than expected at just 4,400 in Q1, and the company grew the lease fleet by another 1,200 units to 16,700 units. The backlog remains strong, though it has come down in recent years. Examining the backlog helps project some revenue and cash flow over the next year. In Q1, the new railcar backlog was 16,300, valued at $2.2 billion. The backlog has for the most part been in the 20,000s on average over time, so this is well below the historical average.

Dividends And Repurchases

Holding a house position for the long term is a good approach here given the dividends and repurchases. The Board just declared a quarterly dividend of $0.32 per share, payable on February 17, 2026, to shareholders of record as of January 27, 2026. This is Greenbrier's 47th consecutive quarterly dividend and is $0.02 higher than the quarterly dividend from when we last covered the company. Management also bought back 303,000 shares in the quarter for $13 million and has $65 million remaining in the authorization.

Forward View

Looking ahead, the company expects 17,500-20,500 deliveries of railcars for the year. This is, of course, down from historical averages. Revenue should come in between $2.7 billion and $3.2 billion, while capex will be down to $285 million, while the company plans to sell another $165 million of its equipment this year. Assuming we see margins in the mid- to high-teens for the year, we see EPS coming in between $3.85 and $5.00. So, EPS trends are lower, though value persists. We like holding a house position.

data.title: SMX Has Promising Traceability Tech, But Hold For Now
data.description:
SMX (Security Matters) Public Limited Company (SMX) is a company that produces invisible molecular markers. These are used in materials to provide them with a traceable identity that can be linked to a digital record that can be hosted on a device, on private servers, on the cloud, or in a blockchain ledger. This identity supports control across the lifecycle of products, including recycling. Recently, the company extended its circular-rubber program into latex and rubber gloves, in a pilot stage initiative. But, overall, I don’t see enough tangible value to justify a bullish rating at this stage, which is why I rate SMX a “Hold” for now.

Molecular Markers

SMX (Security Matters) Public Limited Company is a technology company that delivers brand protection, authentication, and track-and-traced products for businesses. SMX was incorporated in 2022, initially as Empatan plc. The company is the product of a de-SPAC merger between Lionheart III Corp. (LION), which was the SPAC company, and Security Matters, which previously was traded on the Australian Securities Exchange (ASX) as Security Matters Limited. The merger closed in March 2023, and SMX is also listed in Dublin, Ireland.

As a quick overview, SMX offers an identification system with three main components: markers, readers with a proprietary algorithm, and a digital platform. The markers are physical and chemical. They are made of embedded sub-molecular particles that can be applied to solids, liquids, or gases in a permanent or removable fashion. SMX’s Markers are unique, hard to duplicate, and they are verifiable even after industrial processes where other labels are lost. Designated readers detect the markers, and the algorithm identifies and validates the marked material or product.

Ultimately, SMX’s digital platform or ledger captures the data for the product and stores it locally on the readers' private servers, on cloud infrastructure, or on a blockchain digital ledger to preserve integrity and custody. This is how SMX’s products solve authentication and anti-counterfeit problems. Also, these products give materials memory to monitor all the events in the supply chain to enhance its integrity, quality assurance, and brand protection. Additionally, SMX’s offerings allow recycling and plastics identification, detecting the markers for better sorting and higher-quality recyclate.

Safer Circular Recovery

Moreover, SMX announced that they’re expanding their industrial rubber traceability platform into the latex and rubber gloves market. SMX called it the sixth application of its rubber program, aiming to make gloves traceable at the material level to facilitate recovery and recycling. Glove recycling is difficult because they often have contamination risks from biological or chemical factors.

This matters because gloves are not easy to classify due to the difficulty in distinguishing in a reliable way and at scale among latex, nitrile, neoprene, or blends. As a result, most end up in landfills or incineration. SMX’s system delivers memory to the materials that persists across its use and processing, maintaining digital records to allow classification and the recovery of materials when it is safe. With that in mind, note that the glove industry has high product volumes, with a market estimated at $13.8 billion in 2024. But this TAM could exceed $21.6 billion by 2030, with annual consumption exceeding 330 billion units.

Therefore, this type of product volume justifies that even small improvements in recovery could translate into meaningful gains. However, according to the press release, this initiative is still a pilot and the building of an ecosystem, not yet contracts with revenue. Similarly, previous applications of SMX’s circular-rubber program include bicycle tires, engine mounts, internal rubber components, vehicle tires, and truck tires. Most of these initiatives are still in pilots, trials, and validation runs, with no clear evidence of full commercial-scale rollouts.

Interestingly, the EU’s Ecodesign for Sustainable Products Regulation (ESPR) introduces the Digital Product Passport (DPP) as a digital record to identify products, with product-specific requirements progressively with fuller rollout toward 2030. I believe this new regulation could become a tailwind for SMX because it overlaps with its focus on materials traceability and circularity. Time will tell if SMX capitalizes on this type of regulatory initiative going forward.

Valuation And Risk Analysis

In any event, from a valuation perspective, SMX now trades at a $295.5 million market cap. The latest information we have is from their unaudited financials as of June 2025, which show their balance sheet holds $750.0 thousand in cash against approximately $7.7 million in financial debt (loans and convertible notes), aside from other regular operating liabilities. However, the majority of SMX’s assets are in goodwill ($26.1 million) and intangibles ($10.5 million). That’s how SMX’s book value reached $38.9 million, which would imply a high P/B of 7.6 using those figures. For comparison, the sector’s median P/B is much lower at around 3.7.

Moreover, the same proforma financials (June 2025) show SMX burned through approximately $4.2 million during 1H2025. Note that I got that figure by simply adding its cash flows from operations and CAPEX. This would suggest a very limited cash runway at the time. That’s why SMX had to do its recent equity raises. By December 2025, SMX disclosed that it’s going to raise $16.5 million through convertible promissory notes. So, adding that to their 1H2025 cash balances, it would imply a cash runway of approximately 2.1 years, which is much more manageable.

Additionally, we know SMX secured a $100 million equity line that functions similar to an ATM. So, in total, SMX could access up to $116.5 million in additional capital if needed. However, I think the best approach is to simply work with the recent $16.5 million in promissory notes. That would imply a total debt of roughly $24.2 million and $17.3 million in cash. Thus, I estimate SMX’s EV at around $302.4 million, which would still place it decisively in highly speculative microcap territory.

But, despite being a relatively tiny company, the stock does have a very healthy average trading volume of 2.5 million shares. At the current PPS of $28.00, that implies reasonably good liquidity of $70.0 million for a microcap. In that sense, I would say this name will likely have manageable slippage costs and volatility relative to most microcaps, which is a plus that does derisk its investment profile to some extent.

Conclusion: Not Worth The Risk For Now

Overall, I feel SMX remains in pilot stages with a promising tracking technology that hasn’t found its product-market fit yet. Unfortunately, SMX doesn’t have any meaningful revenues today to justify its current valuation. And I believe its current market cap is mostly just pricing in the optionality from its sizeable TAM and recent capital infusion. But, from an investment angle, I don’t see any meaningful revenue milestone in the near term that could warrant a bullish rating. Similarly, I think SMX probably has enough access to capital to work on its vision, which could pay off eventually if it succeeds. Thus, on balance, I lean towards a “Hold” rating for now.

data.title: Why Venezuela's Political Transition Has Left Oil Markets Largely Unmoved
data.description:
By Łukasz Zembik

Global oil prices have reacted only marginally to Venezuela’s political transition, reflecting a market consensus that the country will not deliver a meaningful increase in crude supply in the near future. While Venezuela holds vast oil reserves, investors remain focused on practical constraints - limited production capacity, deteriorated infrastructure, and weak investment economics - which prevent any rapid supply response.

Production capacity remains severely limited

At present, Venezuela’s effective production capacity is estimated at around 1 million barrels per day, only slightly above current output and far below the roughly 2.5 million barrels per day produced a decade ago. Years of mismanagement, underinvestment, and international sanctions have left the oil sector unable to scale up quickly. Recent U.S. actions have reinforced these limits: sanctions on shipping reduced exports to about 500,000 barrels per day, forcing production cuts as storage filled up. Even proposals for the U.S. to purchase oil held in floating storage would ease logistical pressures rather than unlock new supply.

Weak investment economics and high costs

Some modest recovery is possible if sanctions are eased and the United States becomes a stable buyer. Smaller, risk-tolerant producers could restart marginal fields, potentially lifting output by around 300,000 barrels per day over the next two to three years. This would bring production toward roughly 1.4 million barrels per day - still insufficient to influence a global market consuming more than 100 million barrels per day.

Structural and economic barriers remain substantial. Venezuela’s heavy, high-sulfur crude requires costly extraction, blending, and specialized refining, resulting in persistent discounts to benchmarks such as WTI. Estimates suggest breakeven prices could approach USD 80 per barrel, making large-scale investment unattractive at current price levels. Rebuilding the sector would require around USD 100 billion in total investment, with annual spending of roughly USD 12 billion needed for decades to return production toward 3 million barrels per day - likely not before around 2040.

Long-term strategic value, limited near-term impact

Political risk further discourages major oil companies. A history of nationalization, contract revisions, and unresolved arbitration claims has left international producers wary of committing capital without strong legal and fiscal guarantees. While Chevron (CVX) has maintained a limited presence, most large Western firms remain cautious.

Against this backdrop, U.S. interest in Venezuela’s oil appears driven less by immediate market needs and more by long-term strategy. With U.S. shale output expected to plateau later this decade and other Western hemisphere producers peaking in the early 2030s, future supply constraints are becoming a growing concern. Global oil demand continues to rise, and maintaining current production levels will require massive ongoing investment worldwide.

In this context, Venezuela represents a long-duration strategic option rather than a near-term supply shock. Its oil resources could matter in the 2030s if prices rise significantly and political conditions stabilize, but for now, structural limitations explain why markets have largely shrugged off recent developments.

Technical perspective

At present, Brent crude prices are rising dynamically and approaching the area of USD 63.5 per barrel. For now, the USD 59 level has proven to be a fairly strong support zone, with lows in this area recorded in mid-December 2025 as well as earlier in April and May. This support has so far prevented a deeper decline, and with a relatively high probability, prices may continue to move higher in the short term. Technical resistance is located around USD 65-65.5 per barrel, with the next major resistance near USD 70.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Beyond Meat: The Situation Remains Difficult
data.description:
The Beyond Meat investment thesis

Beyond Meat, Inc. (BYND) is a company that has been seen as having a lot of potential in the past. The trend towards healthy eating has brought the meat substitute industry into focus, but unfortunately, progress has stalled somewhat. Now, the main topic regarding Beyond Meat is its massive debt.

Unfortunately, the developments of the last few weeks, as well as the revision of the debt restructuring documents, lead me to keep my Sell rating.

What was my previous coverage like

My last two articles have largely focused on the debt restructuring at Beyond Meat and the fact that this will probably lead to significant dilution. Although debt restructuring was the right move to secure the company's survival in the short term, whether the turnaround will work remains to be seen.

Beyond Meat's mixed shelf offering

On December 23, Beyond Meat issued a mixed shelf offering. A mixed shelf offering is when a company registers securities that can be sold at a later time. The advantage is that it is now possible to issue new shares or take on debt quickly without a lot of paperwork. These shares can now remain on the shelf for up to three years and can be issued whenever the market outlook is positive.

Based on this, one can assume that the dilution will continue, but I think that's something that was expected.

Some observations about the debt restructuring from the end of last year

One point I did not mention before is that the new 2030 loan is secured, and it is a PIK loan. PIK stands for “payment in kind.”

A PIK, or payment in kind, is a type of high-risk loan or bond that allows borrowers to pay interest with additional debt, rather than cash. That makes it an expensive, high-risk financing instrument since the size of the debt may increase quickly, leaving lenders with big losses if the borrower is unable to pay back the loan.

Source: Wikipedia

The above passage would be for a standard PIK loan. But as the PIK at Beyond Meat is a PIK toggle, there is a slight difference.

PIK toggles, also known as "pay if you want," are slightly less risky than PIKs, as borrowers pay interest in cash and may "toggle" to payment in kind at the discretion of the borrower ("pay if you want").

Source: Wikipedia.

This means that with a PIK toggle, it is initially assumed that interest payments will be made in cash and not through the issuance of new debt.

Sometimes, the borrower may also be able to PIK some portion of the interest (usually half) while paying the rest in cash; at times, only some of the interest may be paid in kind and the rest is cash-only.

Source: Wikipedia.

But it also seems possible to pay part of the interest with normal PIK, so by taking on new debt, and the rest with cash. Often, using PIK results in an additional charge of a few basis points, which increases the interest rate. I just wanted to mention these PIK loans because they are rare and easy to overlook.

It will be important to see exactly how the interest payments are made now and at what intervals. This could provide valuable insights for the future.

Strike Price Warrants

At the end of last year, the strike price for Unprocessed Food's warrants was also reduced from $3.26 to $1.95. My assessment of the situation is that a share price of $3.26 is probably unlikely in the near future, which is why the strike price was lowered. The new strike price is also approximately in line with the conversion rate that we will see in the next section.

Conversion rates for senior secured notes due in 2030

Beyond Meat, Inc. (NASDAQ: BYND) (the “Company” or “Beyond Meat”), a leader in plant-based meat, today announced the initial conversion rate for its 7.00% Convertible Senior Secured Second Lien PIK Toggle Notes due 2030 (the “2030 Convertible Notes”) will be 572.7784 shares of the Company’s common stock per $1,000 principal amount of the 2030 Convertible Notes, which represents a conversion price of approximately $1.7459 per share of common stock.

Source: Beyond Meat.

What I found interesting about this announcement is that the conversion price is around $1.7. So, we have a reference point at which it could become interesting, as well as a point that could potentially limit the upside. But I don't think the upside is the main topic at the moment, as the shares are currently trading at $1.03 (4:00 PM, 01/08/26), so it will be interesting to see if the shares fall below the $1 threshold at any point. If a company has an average share price of less than $1 for 30 consecutive days, Nasdaq will issue a deficiency notice. The company then has 180 days to resolve the price deficiency.

Valuation

In my last article, I estimated the cash value per share at $0.125. If we add on top of that the value of the machines and the brand, I would estimate that a fair value is currently around $0.50 to $0.75. However, we must consider that the cash value per share will probably decrease each quarter, as the cash burn is still ongoing. This means that the $0.50 to $0.75 range could also be too high and require significant adjustment in the next quarter.

Conclusion

In conclusion, I would say that the current situation is difficult, as the level of debt restricts the scope for maneuver and makes a turnaround more challenging. However, I don't think a turnaround is impossible. But the risk is still too high for me personally. So I have to stick with my Sell rating.

data.title: Ondas Holdings: The New Defense Drone Darling Is Up 43% YTD
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

Yesterday, Ondas Holdings (NASDAQ:ONDS) stock surged some 15%+, reaching close to its all-time highs (from 2021, since it IPO’ed) on the back of US President Donald Trump’s proposal for a massive increase to the FY27 defense budget to $1.5T. On top of that, news also emerged that the company was selected as a prime contractor for Israel’s “Drone Hives” program to secure its borders using autonomous unmanned aircraft systems.

Ondas Holdings develops and produces highly functional, industrial-grade autonomous drone platforms under its OAS (Ondas Autonomous Systems) business division with a mission to protect and secure critical assets, locations, and populations and support the homeland defense of allied nations.

Amid a favorable macro environment of expanding defense budgets in the US and NATO (ex. US) amid rising geopolitical tensions and “buy American” mandates, there has been growing demand for AI-enabled autonomous field-ready drone platforms.

This has led to Ondas seeing the revenue of their OAS business segment balloon by 900% YoY in their latest Q3 FY25 earnings with a robust backlog ahead. This is the case as the company simultaneously pursues strategic acquisitions to broaden their commercial reach and strengthen their product ecosystem to double down on their “system of systems” model that unites air and ground sensing and communications into an integrated autonomy ecosystem.

The stock was up close to 300% last year, while YTD it is now up close to 43%.

Having said that, the stock is by no means “cheap,” as it is trading at a forward price-to-sales ratio of 120, with no profitability, making it a highly speculative pick.

However, given its balance sheet strength and revenue momentum, investors could look to initiate a small position in the company when the daily RSI on the technical chart normalizes in the 60’s range normalizes. In the meantime, I will rate the stock a “hold.”

The Macro Tailwind Supporting Ondas Holdings

Yesterday, US President Donald Trump called for defense spending to be raised to $1.5T, a 50% increase over the current budget.

"After long and difficult negotiations with Senators, Congressmen, Secretaries, and other Political Representatives, I have determined that, for the Good of our Country, especially in these very troubled and dangerous times, our Military Budget for the year 2027 should not be $1 Trillion Dollars, but rather $1.5 Trillion Dollars," Trump wrote on Truth Social Thursday evening.

This, President Trump claimed, would allow them to build the “Dream Military” to keep the country safe and secure. While there are doubts as to whether the proposed increase in defense spending for 2027 would lead to even larger US fiscal deficits, it certainly led to a 15% jump in Ondas Holdings' stock price, sending it close to its all-time high from early 2021.

In December, Stifel had published a research report, where they predicted that 2026 will be the year of drones, given the significant legislative and executive actions that have been taken throughout 2025 to lay the groundwork for this shift.

One of the biggest catalysts is the "Big Beautiful Bill," which explicitly allocates billions in new funding for drone infrastructure, which Ondas management also acknowledged in their 2025 Investor Presentation. Per management commentary, the bill allocates a total of $21.2B to programs relevant to Ondas’s capabilities and services for fence and security applications in the US. This is how they broke it down below.

  1. $4.2B+ in drone and C-UAS-specific development and industrial base support
  2. $1.7B+ in AI, autonomy, and ISR testbed development
  3. $2.9B+ in border and maritime UAS and sensor deployment (CBP, USCG)
  4. $12.5B+ in FAA investments that underpin BVLOS (Beyond Visual Line of Sight) and autonomous airspace integration.

Not only that, but NATO’s (ex. US) recent commitment to increase defense and related infrastructure spending to 5% of GDP by 2035 also implies a 2.5x growth in outlays, translating to over $700B annually across Europe and Canada, providing an extremely favorable macro backdrop for Ondas Holdings.

What Does Ondas Holdings Do?

Ondas Holdings provides autonomous drone and private wireless solutions for industrial and government operations. It operates its business through two segments that include 1) Ondas Autonomous Systems (OAS) and 2) Ondas Networks.

In this post, we are going to focus our attention on the company’s OAS business unit, which is responsible for developing and producing industrial-grade autonomous drone platforms that include their Optimus System and Iron Drone Raider that defend against hostile drone threats, collect and process data, and provide essential intelligence that helps their customers to efficiently and securely execute their operations.

While Optimus and Iron Drone Raider form the core of their Aerial Drone Infrastructure and Solutions, one of the main strategic priorities of the company is centered on building a “system of systems” model that unites air, ground sensing, and communications into an integrated autonomy ecosystem, unlocking significant revenue and profitability upside. One of the key mechanisms to broadening their commercial reach and strengthening their product ecosystem has been through strategic acquisitions.

In the last several months, they have added 5 new companies that bring critical capabilities, technologies, and customer relations to the platform. In the meantime, as their pipeline matures and broadens, the management has guided for 20+ M&A transactions in the coming years with $500M+ in revenue potential from these acquisitions.

Here is what management said about how their acquisitions are helping them broaden out their platform capabilities.

“With SPO, we are now engaged in critical components for missiles and advanced drone systems, reinforcing our access to the defense supply chain. Apeiro Motion marks our entry into ground robotics and payload systems, a key capability for broader defense and maneuvering forces operating in complex terrain. Foreign Defense expands our subground and engineering platforms, enabling OAS to participate in land clearance and mining operations, which are essential to modern defense missions.

In the counter-UAS domain, our primary focus area, we added Insight Intelligent sensors, which delivers electro-optical and AI-driven identification of [indiscernible] drones. And Sentrycs, which we entered into a definitive agreement with Cyber-over-RF technology provides one of the most effective and precise counter-UAS solutions available, capable of naturalizing threats with minimal collateral impact. And with Zickel, which we acquired in July, we have further strengthened our elite engineering team, adding important capabilities valued by our defense customers.”

Assessing The Investment Opportunity

In Ondas’s latest Q3 FY25 earnings, revenues grew a whopping 580% YoY to $10.1M, up from $1.5M last year. This was almost entirely driven by OAS, which saw their revenues grow 900% YoY to $10M, given the strength in deliveries from their Optimus system and Iron Drone.

Meanwhile, their backlog also grew to $23.3M as their pipeline matures from winning new contracts. When including another $18M from their acquisitions that have either closed or are pending closure in Q4, their backlog figure almost doubles to $40M.

This led management to revise their full-year revenue guidance higher by 44% to $36M while setting an initial revenue target for FY26 at $110M. Meanwhile, when it comes to consensus estimates, the magnitude of upward revision in forward revenue growth estimates is truly spectacular over the last 6 months, as can be seen below.

Meanwhile, when it comes to profitability, gross margins grew to 26%, driven by higher-margin product revenue at OAS compared to lower-margin service and subscription revenue in Q3 of 2024. However, management warned that gross margins can be volatile on a quarter-to-quarter basis due to “revenue levels that reflect the early stages of platform adoption, certain fixed service costs reflected in our cost of goods sold, and shifts in revenue mix between product development, product development, and services revenue.”

Meanwhile, when it comes to operating margin, the company is deeply in the red, and it is expected to remain that way at least for the next five years, as the company continues to pursue aggressive growth strategies on both R&D and Marketing & Sales to capture market share.

However, I will admit that it is a highly speculative company, where we can’t implement traditional valuation methods to assess price targets. At the moment, the stock is trading at a forward price-to-sales ratio of 120, which is not “cheap” by any means.

In fact, the closest comparison that I can think of would be Palantir (NASDAQ:PLTR), which trades at a forward price-to-sales ratio of 98. Having said that, the projected forward revenue growth rate of Palantir is slower compared to Ondas, while the former is also profitable, unlike the latter.

Investment Risks And Conclusions

Like I said earlier, Ondas is a highly speculative name at the moment with a steep forward price-to-sales ratio and no path to immediate profitability, which makes it prone to severe volatility.

Having said that, its balance sheet is in great shape, as it raised $855M since June to support their growth plan, which gives them a competitive advantage, allows them to scale efficiently, and captures market share in fast-growing markets that they are targeting. Meanwhile, their debt load has also declined significantly, as it converted a majority of its outstanding convertible debt into equity throughout 2025, which reduced its total debt from over $55M in Q3 FY24 to a little over $11M a year later.

Therefore, if the company continues to expand their pipeline by winning large contracts in the coming quarters, the stock can continue to go higher, assuming the overall macro conditions remain anchored.

However, investors need to take into account additional risks from shareholder dilution as well as competitive risks from larger and deeper-pocketed players like Lockheed Martin (NYSE:LMT), Northrop Grumman (NYSE:NOC) as well as emerging players like AeroVironment (NASDAQ:AVAV).

At the moment, the stock is starting to look a bit extended on the technical chart with the daily RSI close to 77, so interested investors should wait for a normalization in RSI levels to the 60’s range before choosing to buy a “small” position in the company.

In the meantime, I will rate the company a “hold” at current levels.

data.title: Nu Holdings: The Flywheel Thesis At Fair Value
data.description:
Company Overview:

NU Holdings (NU), the parent company of Nubank, operates the largest digital bank in Latin America, with 114 million customers. NU’s fully mobile model, with no physical branches, enables significant operating leverage and lower cost-to-serve than traditional incumbents. The bank was founded in Brazil in 2013, expanding to Mexico in 2019 and Colombia in 2020, followed by an IPO in 2021. NU’s primary credit card product serves as a point of entry, offering low-cost credit card products aimed at underbanked/unbanked individuals, alongside a suite of financial services, including personal (including payroll) loans, savings accounts, insurance, and investment management.

The bank utilizes proprietary data to underwrite traditionally too-risky customers, enabling astronomical growth into underserved, developing markets. NUAccount’s high-yield value proposition, made possible by the bank’s efficient cost structure, allows NU to acquire low-cost funding in new and existing markets. NU is regulated as a payment institution/fintech, not requiring a bank charter, and paving the way for faster, cheaper expansion.

Theses:

NU’s unparalleled cost structure, cross-selling strategy, and data advantage have and will position it to not only capture the 26% of Latin America that is unbanked but also upend traditionally banked individuals. Particularly, by attacking the poorer, unbanked populations using superior data-driven, idiosyncratic underwriting, NU captures a massive segment. By not capturing market share from traditional financial institutions, NU can hastily and meaningfully cross-sell once individuals are onboarded. The entry-level purple card takes less than 20 minutes to register for, has no income or transaction history requirement, and uses a base $60/month limit that increases as the user pays bills and deposits paychecks into a checking account. The underserved nature of NU’s target demographic creates unparalleled loyalty, as it is most customers’ first exposure to financial services. Despite an overall higher NPL ratio, the risk-adjusted returns are strong, driven by NU’s data advantage.

Furthermore, the average revenue per active customer (ARPAC) maturity runway in Brazil, combined with triple-digit percentage customer growth in Colombia and Mexico, creates massive earnings growth through margin expansion and customer acquisition. Using current ARPAC in all three countries compared to ARPAC amongst mature cohorts, a 300% monetization runway still exists, providing a massive growth driver. NU management doesn’t need to pursue value-destructive growth, and can reliably recycle earnings back into growth or begin to return money to shareholders.

Next, strong “word of mouth” organic growth and low customer acquisition and servicing costs support persistently high margins. With a lifetime value to customer acquisition cost ratio of over 30, combined with a monthly cost to serve of $.80 and a new customer ARPAC of $12/month, aggressive expansion pays off quickly with unit economics improving through cross-selling maturity. Finally, NU’s efficient scale, vertical integration, and data network effects create strong moats and a “flywheel” effect that enables compounding growth.

With >114 million customers, NU’s rich database already drives underwriting advantages, but each marginal customer, especially in new markets such as Mexico and Colombia, compounds this edge. Furthermore, with an integrated platform primed for cross-selling, product expansion across financial services, either through M&A, partnerships (such as the current insurance distribution agreement with Chubb), or internal development (such as expansion into wealth management and high-end credit cards) creates a natural value proposition to already loyal customers.

Another one of NU’s notable strengths is a unique combination of outsider and insider-oriented management. Founder and CEO David Osorno worked at Goldman Sachs, Morgan Stanley, and General Atlantic prior to founding NU. During his time as a partner at Sequoi, working in Brazil, he noticed the stagnant, oligopolistic, and high-fee financial services sector was primed for disruption. His co-founder and current CGO, Cristina Junqueira, was a senior executive in the credit card arm of Itau Unibanco, the largest Brazilian bank at the time of NU’s founding. The combination of Junqueira’s domain expertise and Osorno’s start-up and financial experience led to NU’s novel and captivating value proposition and blistering growth.

Model Results:

I estimate a target price of $17.75, representing a slight 0.5% discount to 1/7 market open. While this would typically justify a hold rating, the structure of my model suggests that the current stock price accurately discounts growth in existing markets but does not consider added optionality. Specifically, the model is inherently conservative, only forecasting growth in existing markets (Mexico, Colombia, and Brazil). I forecast terminal penetration rates in each market relative to Brazil, and ramp ARPAC up to $350 at the end of the forecast period, reflective of existing figures, but not considering probable future cross-selling opportunities. I pursue this framework as it is difficult to accurately forecast growth in new, unexplored markets (NU has an international expansion strategy but no concrete plans for additional specific countries at the moment, beyond Colombia and Mexico, and a nascent operation in the US) and to value future cross-selling opportunities. Furthermore, maintain CET1 targets at a conservative 2x regulatory minimum, reflective of management’s most recent stated goals.

Strong adherence to Basel III guidance creates ample liquidity, combating fears of under-regulation and providing room for increased future operational efficiency should management adopt a more aggressive capital adequacy stance. Modeling using government and zero-coupon swap curves, I forecast NU’s deposit funding advantage eroding slightly over the forecast period, contributing to a terminal 13% NIM, or a 6% compression from today.

Next, I forecast NU’s mature loan portfolio mix to favor secured loans, contributing to a decline from 26.8% to 21.7% blended yield. These changes are not negative, only reflective of natural shifts away from high-yield unsecured instruments. Consistent 5-10% customer growth and consistently increasing cohort monetization support strong bottom-line growth.

Furthermore, NU’s strategy of aggressively pricing in new markets, both through higher-yielding saving accounts and lower-cost credit products, counteracts NIM compression arising from competition, and still leaves the bank with significantly higher baseline margins compared to traditional competitors. Continuing operating leverage supports G&A as a % of revenue, shrinking from 11% to 4% over the forecast. My 11% COE reflects a combination of Brazilian ERP estimates sourced from Aswath Damodaran and a beta of 0.93, slightly below market to reflect NU’s evolution into a mature fintech over the long 15-year forecast period.

Risks and Mitigants:

If NU fails to capture a significant market share in Mexico and Colombia, or bleeds market share in Brazil, then its growth engine fails, and the market’s current valuation appears significantly overstated. However, the bank’s 98% retention rates (extrapolated from monthly churn), unheard of in financial services, combined with growth to 10 million customers in five years in Mexico and Colombia, and existing data advantages, deter competitive rivalry and support terminal assumptions. A second risk is that NU has no runway left in Brazil, already serving 50% of the adult population.

While customer growth will undoubtedly slow in the coming years, blended ARPAC sits at $100, meaning that increasing monetization of this massive existing customer base creates long-term value alongside organic customer growth in new markets outside Brazil. Lastly, a slew of new fintech/digital bank entrants aim to displace NU’s significant market share. However, these firms, such as Mercado Pago and PicPay, cannot compete with NU’s vertical integration, only serving niches. Additionally, the aforementioned data moats create structural difficulties in capturing market share.

Conclusion:

NU represents one of the rare international growth stories that has shown a durable double and triple-digit growth, and is primed to continue intense expansion. Furthermore, NU's early entry, combined with a unique data-based approach to bank underserved segments, allowed it to gain an advantage in a crowded fintech space. As customer growth drives the entrenchment of the bank's data moat and product expansion drives further monetization of a loyal customer base, NU is primed to expand its domination of the Latin American fintech space.

data.title: ACWV: Effective Risk Reduction Strategy
data.description:
ACWV Strategy

iShares MSCI Global Min Vol Factor ETF (ACWV) seeks to provide exposure to global equities with lower volatility relative to the broad market. ACWV was launched on 10/18/2011, has a portfolio of 380 stocks, a 30-day SEC yield of 1.83% and a total expense ratio of 0.20%. Distributions are paid semi-annually.

As described in the prospectus by iShares, the underlying index starts from the MSCI All Country World Index (“MSCI ACWI”) and optimizes constituent weights with a rules-based methodology “to construct a portfolio of lowest absolute volatility using its multi-factor risk model”. Additionally, some constraints on liquidity, financial leverage, constituent and sector weights are enforced. The index is reconstituted on a quarterly basis. The portfolio turnover rate was 27% in the most recent fiscal year. I will use as a benchmark the parent index, represented by iShares MSCI ACWI ETF (ACWI).

Portfolio

The fund has 59% of assets in U.S.-domiciled companies. Japan comes in second position with 10%, followed by China (6%), India (5%) and Taiwan (4%). Other countries are below 2%. Geopolitical risk related to China is moderate: the aggregate weight of China, Taiwan, and Hong Kong is 11.5%.

The portfolio has a focus on technology (22.9%) and notable exposure in financials (15%), healthcare (14.6%) and communication (12.8%). Other sectors are below 10%. Despite concentration in a few sectors, ACWV shows better sector diversification than its parent index.

Company-specific risk is low. Indeed, the top 10 holdings, listed in the next table, represent only 12.1% of asset value and the largest position weighs 1.54%. Once again, ACWV is better diversified than ACWI, where the top 10 names weight 24.3%, and the heaviest one 4.75%. Nine of the top 10 issuers are U.S. companies.

Performance

ACWV has underperformed ACWI by 2.5% annualized since 10/25/2011. Lower volatility (measured by standard deviation of monthly returns in the table below) results in a risk-adjusted performance (measured by Sharpe ratio) identical to the benchmark’s.

The annual sum of distributions has increased by 57% between 2015 and 2025, outpacing inflation (about 38%, based on CPI). Nonetheless, it was not a steady uptrend, as plotted below.

Competitors

As far as I know, there is no other global low-volatility ETF (including the U.S., developed and emerging markets). As ACWV has almost 60% of assets in the U.S., I will compare its characteristics with five low-volatility U.S. ETFs:

ACWV is in last position for total return since September 2016, but it is not a dealbreaker. In this time frame, outperformance of the U.S. stock market over international equities and the dollar index uptrend have been a drag for ACWV versus its U.S. peers. These macro trends may stop or even revert in the future (international equity benchmarks have nicely outperformed the U.S. market in 2025). More interestingly, ACWV has the lowest volatility and the shallowest drawdown. It is clearly the best of this list at reducing risk.

Takeaway

iShares MSCI Global Min Vol Factor ETF offers global equities' exposure with lower volatility relative to ACWI. ACWV is better diversified in sectors and holdings than the benchmark and has value characteristics. The fund is on par with ACWI in Sharpe ratio due to lower return. ACWV trails key competitors in return since 2016, partly due to macro trends, but boasts excellent risk reduction capability. ACWI is a good candidate for risk-averse investors seeking international diversification.

data.title: ClearBridge Investments Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
“Party like it’s 1999” is a phrase made famous by the musician Prince’s 1982 song, which experienced a renaissance amid Y2K fears and has since entered the lexicon meaning to celebrate intensely because the future is uncertain. The phrase seems apt to describe the current investment landscape, given the similarities between the late 1990s dot-com bubble and today. These include lofty valuations, strong market momentum and a focus on growth stocks. However, we believe there are several key differences between the present environment and 1999 that will keep the markets moving higher in the year to come, which may be a surprise to many.

1999 vs. 2025: Economic Differences

The first key difference is that the U.S. economy is poised to benefit from both fiscal and monetary stimulus in 2026, a potent combination typically only seen coming out of recessions. The net impulse from the One Big Beautiful Bill (OBBB) is expected to deliver ~1% of GDP this year with supercharged tax refunds providing support to low- and middle-income households.

Tax refunds are typically spent rather than saved, suggesting that much of this cash will make its way back into the economy relatively quickly. The COVID stimulus payments provide a good example of this dynamic, with research from the Peter G. Peterson Foundation showing that households earning below $75,000 spent around 80% of the initial stimulus payments they received. That figure dropped but stayed above 50% for households with over $150,000 in earnings. While the 2026 tax refund bonanza will likely fade in the second half of 2026, the OBBB’s fiscal impact should continue in 2027 and 2028 but at lower levels of support around 0.5% of GDP according to Congressional Budget Office and Wolfe Research estimates.

Exhibit 1: Tax Tailwind

Sources: Wolfe Research, CBO, Macrobond. Based on CBO’s Baseline Budget Projections from January 2025’s report The Budget and Economic Outlook: 2025 to 2035. There is no assurance that any estimate, forecast or projection will be realized.

The benefit from this stimulus should be significant because wage growth — the largest source of spending power for most Americans — has continued to moderate following the post-pandemic spike. Although this moderation has strained lower income cohorts and led to the “K-shaped” economy, it is somewhat encouraging from a macro perspective. Typically, maturing economic expansions see accelerating wages that often spook the Fed into tightening to prevent a wage-price inflationary spiral. This, in turn, can choke off economic growth and help set the stage for a recession. However, this dynamic is not in place today, which marks a second key difference between the present and 1999.

Exhibit 2: Wage Trend, Expansion’s Friend

Gray shading marks recessionary periods. As of Dec. 16, 2025, latest available as of Dec. 31, 2025. Sources: US Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS), NBER, Macrobond.

Cooling wages have resulted in the Wage Growth indicator on the ClearBridge Recession Risk Dashboard remaining in solid green territory, which is also where the overall signal continues to reside. The dashboard did see two changes in December: ISM New Orders dropped back to red after the second consecutive monthly reading below 48, while the Yield Curve improved to green as it steepened above 50 basis points. Housing Permits and Retail Sales remain on hold due to lingering delays related to the U.S. government shutdown in October and November, but these issues should hopefully be resolved in the coming weeks.

Exhibit 3: U.S. Recession Dashboard

‘Not Available’ reflects data that has not been updated due to the government shutdown. Data as of Dec. 31, 2025. Sources: BLS, Federal Reserve, Census Bureau, ISM, BEA, American Chemistry Council, American Trucking Association, Conference Board, Bloomberg, CME, FactSet and Macrobond. The ClearBridge Recession Risk Dashboard was created in January 2016. References to the signals it would have sent in the years prior to January 2016 are based on how the underlying data was reflected in the component indicators at the time.

The trend of moderating wage gains over the past few years has stood in stark contrast to an economy that has continued to deliver solid growth. Gross domestic product (GDP) has grown by an average of 2.8% on a real basis since the end of the first quarter of 2023. This momentum has shown little sign of slowing recently, with the third quarter of 2025 coming in at 4.3%. At the same time, the unemployment rate has risen over a full percentage point to 4.6% from the 3.4% lows in April 2023. As a result, some observers are blaming the rise of artificial intelligence (AI) for recent labor market weakness.

AI does appear to be contributing toward softer hiring in the technology industry and for entry-level roles in particular. However, a broader review shows that occupations where AI adoption is the lowest have seen the greatest rise in unemployment, suggesting that other factors have been driving labor weakness. Job growth for industries more rapidly adopting AI is actually positive, driven by AI augmentation as opposed to substitution — a dynamic we explored in last month’s blog.

Exhibit 4: Lower AI Adoption, Weaker Labor

Data as of Nov. 30, 2025; latest available as of Dec. 31, 2025. Sources: Wolfe Research, Haver Analytics.

AI adoption has been advancing at an extremely fast pace compared to past innovations. Positive technological breakthroughs typically lead to a pickup in productivity and a drop in inflation (or even deflation). This dynamic usually takes time to bear out as was the case during the 1990s. Given the rapid adoption of AI over the past few years, however, we believe there’s a strong possibility this lag could be compressed. If this proves true, the risk to inflation could end up being to the downside, not the upside, in 2026.

The “January effect” is likely to be larger than normal once again this year. However, a pickup in productivity combined with moderating wage gains, a softening labor market, weaker shelter prices and lower commodity costs could all push inflation lower as we move deeper into 2026. Further disinflation — the aforementioned scenario largely represents a continuation of 2025 trends — would likely be bullish for financial markets as it would allow for further Fed easing should employment growth remain lackluster (50k-75k jobs per month).

Another 2025 trend likely to continue in the new year is the rapid growth of AI capital expenditures (capex) as the AI infrastructure buildout continues. Despite fears that AI capex has reached bubble territory, current levels of spending are well below the peak seen during prior innovative technological cycles in the U.S., as a percentage of GDP. For example, AI investment accounts for about 1% of the U.S. economy today compared to 3% during the late 1990s tech/telecom bubble. Should history repeat, AI capex could surprise to the upside in the years to come, providing continued support for both the economy and markets.

Exhibit 5: More AI Capex Ahead

Data as of Oct. 20, 2025; latest available as of Dec. 31, 2025. Source: Bureau of Economic Analysis, Goldman Sachs Global Investment Research.

1999 vs. 2025: Market Differences

The funding source of this capex brings us to the third key difference between the late 1990s and today. Today’s spending is largely financed from corporate free cash flow (FCF), whereas the tech/telecom capex buildout was primarily underwritten in the capital markets through debt and/or equity issuance. Recently, smaller players have begun to increasingly tap debt markets and even the hyperscalers have begun to dip a toe. However, the tech sector’s aggregate capex spending equates to under 40% of FCF, well below the mid-1990s peak of 67%. With today’s leaders flush with cash, so far there has been less of a need to raise capital to fund the AI buildout. Put differently, debt financing will likely become more prevalent in 2026 (and beyond), but we are not currently near concerning levels.

Exhibit 6: Similar Capex Boom, Different Funding

Data as of Dec. 31, 2025. Sources: Datastream, Goldman Sachs Global Investment Research.

A fourth key distinction between the late 1990s and 2025 is what has been powering the equity market higher. During the final surge of the dot-com bubble, P/E expansion was the primary driver of upside, a stark difference from 2025’s improving EPS expectations-powered rally. Within the technology sector specifically, this year’s 23.3% price move was more than fully driven by improving EPS expectations, while multiples contracted and actually detracted from returns. Similarly, over 80% of the S&P 500 Index’s price return in 2025 was driven by improving fundamentals (aka earnings). This illustrates that market participants today are engaging in less speculative behavior as compared with the heyday of the dot-com bubble.

Exhibit 7: Party Like It’s (Not) 1999

Note: Dot Com Peak was March 23, 2000; Contribution to price return based on change in sell-side consensus NTM EPS expectations and NTM P/E. Data as of Dec. 31, 2025. Sources: S&P, FactSet.

An additional sign that investors are behaving less exuberantly than in past bubbles comes from the lower valuation multiples assigned to today’s market darlings. The Magnificent Seven currently trade at 53.3x as a group, in the ballpark of what was seen during the peak of the Nifty Fifty and dot-com bubbles. However, a large portion of the current valuation is driven by Tesla; a “Magnificent Six” or Magnificent Seven ex-Tesla trades at a less lofty 27.4x, which bears far less resemblance to former speculative manias.

Exhibit 8: Are Valuations At Bubble Levels?

*Actual P/E ratios; forward P/Es unavailable for this period. Data as of Dec. 31, 2025. Sources: FactSet, Valuing Growth Stocks: Revisiting the Nifty Fifty; AAII, October 1998, Goldman Sachs. Company references are used for illustrative purposes and should not be construed as an endorsement of sponsorship of Franklin Templeton companies. This information is not intended as an investment recommendation, nor does it constitute investment advice.

Part of this excitement may be due to the Magnificent Seven’s superb earnings over the past three years. However, we continue to anticipate a rotation in leadership as earnings delivery broadens in 2026. Third-quarter earnings season was encouraging on this front, with U.S. companies largely reporting robust earnings and equities beginning to price this dynamic during the fourth quarter of this year. Given the hype around AI, it may surprise some to learn that, among the Magnificent Seven, only Google and Nvidia outperformed the S&P 500 in 2025. We also believe there is further room to run for this trade, which should be a tailwind for active stock pickers who can navigate the concentration risks associated with these recent winners.

Exhibit 9: Mag 7 Losing Altitude

Data as of Dec. 31, 2025. Sources: FactSet, S&P. Magnificent 7 data refers to the following set of stocks: Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), Meta (META), Apple (AAPL), Google parent Alphabet (GOOGL), Nvidia (NVDA), and Tesla (TSLA).

The Market’s Great Paradox

A comprehensive comparison of today’s backdrop and the final hurrah of the dot-com era shows more differences than similarities at this point. While valuations are elevated, we believe equities will “grow into the multiple” in 2026 with strong earnings fueled by ongoing AI capex strength, as well as fiscal and monetary stimulus. Additional upside could come from deregulation, AI-related productivity gains and further labor cost moderation, the latter of which could also open the door to additional Fed easing. Although AI will disrupt labor markets to some degree, we believe the impact will be more like the jobless recovery that followed the dot-com bubble with weaker job gains (50k-75k per month) as opposed to a recession. Additionally, AI should contribute to a disinflationary backdrop that should be bullish for financial assets.

Although it may feel at times like the markets are partying like it’s 1999, we believe the eventual hangover that will follow (such as in 2000-2003) remains further on the horizon. With this in mind, we are reminded of a quote from famous investor William O’Neil: “It is one of the great paradoxes of the stock market that what seems too high usually goes higher and what seems too low usually goes lower.” While the dot-com bubble does have some parallels to the present, it is important to also consider the risks from sitting on the sidelines during periods of large technological change. As such, we remain buyers of dips should any arise in the coming months.

Jeffrey Schulze, CFA, Managing Director, Head of Economic and Market Strategy
